Title: A new synthetic biology approach allows transfer of an entire metabolic pathway from a medicinal plant to a biomass crop

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Core canonical pathway; precursor supply to sesquiterpene branch
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses isoprenoid precursors to produce farnesyl diphosphate (FPP), the precursor for amorpha-4,11-diene synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Precursor for production of the antimalarial drug artemisinin
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Transplastomic leaf tissues under greenhouse growth; expressed from plastid operon
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Stable plastid transformation; metabolite profiling by GC-MS
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FPS was one of the four core enzymes sufficient to establish the canonical artemisinic acid pathway in tobacco chloroplasts. 2. Its plastid expression enabled production of downstream metabolites including amorpha-4,11-diene, artemisinic alcohol and artemisinic acid. 3. It functioned mainly as upstream precursor supplier rather than the identified bottleneck in optimized lines.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enables precursor formation for downstream artemisinic acid biosynthesis in tobacco plastids
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: GC-MS metabolite profiling; qRT-PCR; northern blot
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful as a required upstream module when reconstructing the pathway in plants; should be stacked with ADS, CYP and CPR but is unlikely to be the main optimization target alone
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Pathway installation contributed to mild pigment depletion/growth delay in some operon configurations, likely from isoprenoid precursor drain rather than FPS alone
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Codon optimized for chloroplast expression; arranged in dicistronic plastid operon with CYP

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Core canonical pathway; committed sesquiterpene cyclization step
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Precursor for the antimalarial compound artemisinin
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Cr PpsbA with either T7 Lg10 or Cr LpsbA leader (plastid expression cassette)
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Transplastomic leaf tissues; translation signal differences were tested among operons
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Stable plastid transformation; comparison of operon designs; GC-MS detection of amorpha-4,11-diene and downstream products
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ADS catalyzes the first committed step of the pathway in engineered tobacco plastids. 2. All transplastomic lines expressing ADS accumulated amorpha-4,11-diene, validating ADS activity in plastids. 3. Differences in operon design affected downstream conversion efficiency rather than abolishing ADS-dependent committed-step flux.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Establishes committed flux into artemisinin-pathway sesquiterpenes and enables amorpha-4,11-diene accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Essential entry enzyme for pathway reconstruction; should be combined with optimized downstream oxidation modules for high artemisinic acid yield
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Pathway flux may drain plastid isoprenoid precursors and contribute to reduced carotenoids/chlorophyll in some lines
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper explicitly states ADS catalyzes the first committed step; synthetic operon arrangement and translation control were tested

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes successive oxidations of amorpha-4,11-diene toward artemisinic alcohol/aldehyde/acid intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Artemisinic alcohol and further oxidized intermediates leading to artemisinic acid
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target-product precursors and contributes directly to target formation
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: In A. annua, transcript and protein levels increase in organs/developmental stages where artemisinin synthesis is induced
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Higher transcript accumulation in Nt-AO3 than Nt-AO2 across development; high CYP/CPR ratio correlated with high artemisinic acid
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Stable plastid transformation; northern blot; qRT-PCR; metabolite profiling correlation with CYP/CPR expression ratio
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP71AV1 was essential for oxidation of amorpha-4,11-diene in the engineered pathway. 2. High artemisinic acid accumulation correlated with a high CYP/CPR transcript ratio in transplastomic tobacco. 3. The study inferred that more effective downstream oxidation, especially the final oxidation to artemisinic acid, depends strongly on CYP activity relative to CPR.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes efficient oxidation of amorpha-4,11-diene toward artemisinic acid; higher relative expression increases artemisinic acid accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point suggested for efficient oxidation module
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Plant developmental stage; leaf age; standard greenhouse conditions
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Northern blot; qRT-PCR; GC-MS metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Critical optimization target in pathway engineering; balancing CYP above CPR is recommended to improve flux and reduce CPR-associated inefficiency
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Improper CYP/CPR balance may reduce efficiency; low relative CYP may limit acid production
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper highlights especially the final oxidation step from artemisinic aldehyde to artemisinic acid as requiring an effective monooxygenase

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers electrons from NADPH to CYP71AV1 to support P450-catalyzed oxidation reactions
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Reduced CYP partner enabling oxidation of terpene intermediates
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required cofactor-transfer support for direct target-product formation
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: In A. annua, CPR transcript and protein remain relatively constant across development and organs according to cited comparison discussed in paper
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Higher transcript accumulation in Nt-AO2 than Nt-AO3; lower relative CPR together with higher CYP favored artemisinic acid accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Stable plastid transformation; northern blot; qRT-PCR; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CPR was required in the canonical plastid pathway to support CYP-mediated oxidations. 2. Excess CPR relative to CYP was associated with lower artemisinic acid accumulation. 3. The data support that a high CYP/CPR ratio, rather than high CPR alone, is favorable for efficient flux to artemisinic acid.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports P450-dependent oxidation, but excessive CPR relative to CYP is associated with poorer artemisinic acid production
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not rate limiting alone; stoichiometric balance with CYP is a key control feature
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Plant developmental stage; leaf age; standard greenhouse conditions
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Northern blot; qRT-PCR; GC-MS metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Should be co-expressed with CYP but carefully balanced; avoid overexpression relative to CYP to minimize inefficiency and improve target yield
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Excess CPR can activate oxygen to produce superoxide radicals and waste redox capacity, potentially causing toxicity/inefficiency
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The study discusses CPR-mediated toxicity/inefficient redox use as explanation for poorer performance in low CYP/CPR ratio lines

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Cytochrome b5 family electron transfer protein
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Promotes electron transfer to P450 monooxygenases, thereby potentially enhancing oxidation flux
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Enhanced electron delivery to P450 system
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Chloroplast-targeted in supertransformed plants via RBCS transit peptide
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Combinatorial nuclear supertransformation; genotyping; qRT-PCR; GC-MS metabolic screening
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYB5 was included as an accessory enzyme expected to enhance P450 electron transfer. 2. Presence of CYB5 showed only weak/non-significant correlation with elevated artemisinic acid compared with ALDH1. 3. It was not identified as the predominant bottleneck-relief factor in this study.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Potentially supports oxidation flux, but decisive positive effect on artemisinic acid accumulation was not established
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: GC-MS metabolic screening; genomic PCR; qRT-PCR
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: May be included as auxiliary support in multigene stacks, but lower priority than ALDH1, dxr, ADH1 and proper CYP/CPR balancing
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Accessory gene introduced by COSTREL; no strong standalone statistical support for major effect

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Improves oxidation of artemisinic alcohol to artemisinic aldehyde
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor to target-product-forming oxidation step
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Chloroplast-targeted in supertransformed plants via RBCS transit peptide
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: High in best COSTREL line Nt-AO3-CS180
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Combinatorial supertransformation; genotype-metabolite correlation; qRT-PCR; GC-MS profiling
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ADH1 was introduced to improve oxidation of artemisinic alcohol to artemisinic aldehyde. 2. qRT-PCR supported its contribution in high-producing COSTREL lines, including Nt-AO3-CS180. 3. Its effect was weaker than ALDH1 in correlation analyses but consistent with alleviation of the alcohol-oxidation bottleneck.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Helps reduce artemisinic alcohol bottleneck and promotes increased artemisinic acid production
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Contributes to bottleneck relief but not established as sole key control step
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: GC-MS metabolic screening; genomic PCR; qRT-PCR
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Valuable accessory gene to stack with ALDH1 and dxr for improved oxidation flux and higher artemisinic acid accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Best line expressed ADH1, ALDH1, DBR2 and dxr at high levels

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes artemisinic aldehyde to artemisinic acid; also catalyzes conversion of dihydroartemisinic aldehyde to dihydroartemisinic acid
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Chloroplast-targeted in supertransformed plants via RBCS transit peptide
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: High expression in best COSTREL lines; strongest association with elevated artemisinic acid in Nt-AO3-CS lines
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Combinatorial supertransformation; statistical correlation of transgene presence with metabolite levels; qRT-PCR; GC-MS profiling
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ALDH1 showed the strongest correlation with elevated artemisinic acid levels among accessory genes in Nt-AO3-CS lines. 2. The major pathway bottleneck was oxidation downstream of artemisinic alcohol, and ALDH1 was the predominant relieving factor. 3. High-producing line Nt-AO3-CS180 expressed ALDH1 strongly and accumulated up to 120.4 mg/kg FW artemisinic acid.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Strongly promotes artemisinic acid accumulation by relieving the downstream aldehyde oxidation bottleneck
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control/bottleneck-relief enzyme supported by study
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: GC-MS metabolic screening; genomic PCR; qRT-PCR
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Top-priority engineering target for boosting artemisinic acid in plant chassis; should be stacked with ADH1, dxr and optimized core pathway expression
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No major visible growth penalty attributable to added ALDH1 in best lines
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper explicitly suggests predominant role of ALDH1 in boosting artemisinic acid synthesis

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Branch point to dihydroartemisinic acid/artemisinin route
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces artemisinic aldehyde to dihydroartemisinic aldehyde, diverting flux toward dihydroartemisinic acid and potentially artemisinin
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Diverts flux toward alternate branch leading to artemisinin
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Dihydroartemisinic acid / artemisinin branch, with artemisinic acid also analyzed in study
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Dihydroartemisinic acid is the immediate precursor of artemisinin, an antimalarial drug
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Chloroplast-targeted in supertransformed plants via RBCS transit peptide
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: High in best COSTREL line Nt-AO3-CS180
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Combinatorial supertransformation; qRT-PCR; metabolite profiling by GC-MS
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DBR2 introduces a branch point from artemisinic aldehyde to dihydroartemisinic aldehyde. 2. In engineered tobacco, DBR2 together with ALDH1 supported formation of low amounts of dihydroartemisinic acid, showing plastid functionality. 3. Despite this, no detectable artemisinin accumulated, so DBR2 mainly demonstrated branch competence rather than successful end-product formation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enables diversion toward dihydroartemisinic acid branch but did not yield detectable artemisinin in this system
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Branch-switch enzyme; not decisive for artemisinic acid increase
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: GC-MS metabolic screening; genomic PCR; qRT-PCR
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for future engineering toward artemisinin branch, but additional steps/constraints must be solved because artemisinin was not detected
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May divert flux away from artemisinic acid under some configurations
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The study notes dihydroartemisinic acid was detected only in a few lines, and no artemisinin was detected

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: MEP isoprenoid precursor pathway feeding artemisinic acid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts 1-deoxy-D-xylulose-5-phosphate to 2-C-methyl-D-erythritol 4-phosphate in the MEP pathway, enhancing isoprenoid precursor supply
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Chloroplast-targeted in supertransformed plants via RBCS transit peptide
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: High in best COSTREL line Nt-AO3-CS180
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Combinatorial supertransformation; genotype-metabolite correlation; qRT-PCR; GC-MS profiling
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. dxr was chosen as a key regulatory enzyme of the MEP precursor pathway to increase precursor availability. 2. Correlation and qRT-PCR analyses suggested dxr contributed to increased artemisinic acid content, though less strongly than ALDH1. 3. High expression of dxr was present in the top-producing COSTREL line, supporting precursor-supply improvement as part of yield optimization.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Likely increases artemisinic acid accumulation by improving plastid isoprenoid precursor supply
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key regulatory enzyme of precursor pathway; contributes to control but not sole bottleneck in this study
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: GC-MS metabolic screening; genomic PCR; qRT-PCR
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful support gene for pathway stacking to improve precursor supply alongside downstream bottleneck relief genes
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Could potentially perturb other plastid isoprenoid pools, though no major added phenotype was observed in best COSTREL line
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Source species differs from medicinal plant pathway genes; derived from cyanobacterium Synechocystis
